lesson.menu_title: Small Talk That Works -- The Round Table
lesson.menu_desc: Open, sustain, and close a conversation at an international dinner in Seoul: start with an easy question, react and ask back to keep it going, shift the topic gently with 'by the way', and close warmly -- with the words of small talk and a real dinner conversation with Daniel, a university rector. Vocabulary: introduce, host, guest, impression, topic, conversation, background, polite. Expression: 'By the way.' Idiom: 'Break the ice.' -- 35 slides
lesson.subtitle: Lesson 17 -- Small Talk That Works
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Maria Clara | Lesson 17 -- Small Talk That Works

listenings.text: Good evening, everyone, and welcome to our conference dinner. My name is Sophia, and I am your host tonight. It is a real pleasure to see so many guests from around the world at the same table. Tonight is not about presentations. It is about conversation. So please, introduce yourself to the person next to you. Ask an easy question. Talk about your day, about the city, or about your background. A good dinner starts with good small talk. So relax, enjoy the evening, and make a wonderful first impression.

Hi Maria Clara, it is a quick message before the dinner tonight. I know these events can feel a little scary, so here is one simple idea. Small talk is not a test. It has rules, and you can learn them. Open with an easy question, like: is this your first time here? Then listen, react, and ask one question back. If you want to change the topic, just say: by the way. And please remember, it is polite to ask about the other person too. You are going to be great. See you there.

items.situation: You just sat down next to a guest you do not know. Break the ice and open the conversation.
tips: Hi, I am Maria Clara. Is this your first time in Seoul?
Open with an easy question, not a statement.

items.situation: The guest says the dinner is wonderful. React with a short response, and then ask a question back.
tips: I know what you mean! And what about you, are you enjoying the conference?
React first, then ask back to sustain the conversation.

items.situation: You want to know what the person does. Ask about their background in a polite way.
tips: So, what is your background? What do you do exactly?
background = your job, your studies, and your experience.

items.situation: There is a silence at the table. Change the topic gently.
tips: By the way, how are you finding the city?
By the way = a soft way to move to a new topic.

items.situation: The guest asks about your work at the university. Answer, and ask the same question back.
tips: I lead a team at a federal university. And what about you?
Answer, then return the question to keep it balanced.

items.situation: It is time to leave the table. Close the conversation warmly.
tips: It was really nice talking with you. I hope we can continue later.
Close with a warm thank you, never in silence.
